text: Graded incremental exercise tests elicit an exponential rise in blood lactate concentrations, and shifts in the resulting lactate curve indicate changes in endurance capacity.

The aerobic-anaerobic transition consists of two breakpoints: the intensity at which blood lactate begins to rise above baseline levels, and the maximal lactate steady state (MLSS) — the highest intensity at which lactate production and elimination are in equilibrium.

At least 25 different lactate threshold concepts exist, falling into three categories: fixed blood lactate values, first rise above baseline, and detection of MLSS or distinct inflection in the lactate curve.
The overwhelming majority of 32 studies reported strong linear correlations between lactate thresholds and endurance performance, particularly for running events, indicating high shared variance.

Some lactate threshold concepts can estimate the maximal lactate steady state (MLSS), making them valid for prescribing training intensities, though individual variability between threshold and MLSS is rarely reported.

Several confounding factors influence overall lactate levels, meaning that relying exclusively on the shape of the entire lactate curve cannot precisely estimate the magnitude of endurance gains — discrete lactate thresholds provide more precision.

The aerobic-anaerobic transition framework can be used both for individually assessing endurance performance and for prescribing training intensities, with multiple lactate threshold approaches integrated within it.
